text: The surgeon opens the hernia sac, beginning dissection from the left side and moving circumferentially towards the right crura, exposing the hernia contents.
The attenuated phrenoesophageal ligament is visible in the surgical field, indicating its weakened state.
The surgeon continues dissection in the posterior mediastinum, focusing on separating tissue layers in the area surrounding the hernia sac.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament, revealing the caudate lobe, the inferior vena cava, and the right crura within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection along the right cruse, carefully creating a plane between the right crura and the contents of the hernia sac.

The surgeon dissects in the posterior mediastinum on the right side, enhancing visualization of anatomical structures, with the aim of identifying the convergence of the right and left crura at the base.
The surgeon mobilizes and frees the attachments circumferentially and completely to facilitate the reduction of herniated structures, aiming to minimize the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.
The surgeon focuses on mobilizing and freeing the attachments to the left crus, carefully dissecting to separate these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects in the region near the short gastric vessels, which are visible and may be attenuated during the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the hemicephageal ligament and hernia sac, which appear attenuated during the dissection process.
The surgeon has completely mobilized and freed the attachments to the left crus, facilitating the creation of a retrosophageal window for improved access and visualization.
The surgical instrument is advanced through the retrosophageal window, indicating progression in the dissection and mobilization of the herniated structures.
The instrument emerges at the left upper side, where panrethrin is introduced to facilitate the retraction of the esophagus.
The surgeon continues the dissection to enlarge the retrosophageal window, carefully maneuvering around the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon closes the posterior crural defect, using sutures to approximate the crura and repair the defect visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places multiple figure-of-eight sutures using 0 Ethibond suture material to secure the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon places anterior stitches for an anterior choroplasty, focusing on the area around the esophagus.
The surgeon mobilizes and completely frees the hernia sac, ensuring it does not interfere with the fundoplication process.
The surgeon decides to utilize a biological mesh, which is prepared for placement in the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces the biological mesh and positions it in a reverse letter C shape, ensuring it does not wrap circumferentially around the hernia defect.
The surgeon uses U-stitches to secure the mesh to the underlying anatomical structures, ensuring it remains in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon places a suture on the most prominent portion of the freed cardia, positioning it in a retro-esophageal manner to facilitate the fundoplication.

The surgeon decides to perform a posterior toupee fundoplication after mobilizing the cardia and taking down the short gastric vessels, avoiding a complete wrap to prevent postoperative tension and dysphagia.
The surgeon places multiple sutures between the esophagus and the portion of the cardia to perform a 180-degree wrap, visually securing the structures for the posterior toupee fundoplication.
The surgeon removes the Penrose drain and inspects the hernia closure site for any remaining defects or issues.
The surgeon tucks the mesh into its proper position, ensuring it is securely placed over the hernia site.
